primary_diagnosis.name: Undifferentiated connective tissue disease — flare with severe (secondary) Raynaud's and mild synovitis

pathophysiology_summary: UCTD is an autoimmune state (often ANA-positive) with features of connective tissue disease not meeting criteria for a defined entity; secondary Raynaud's phenomenon causes cold-triggered digital vasospasm with a triphasic colour change (white-blue-red) and painful reperfusion, alongside mild non-deforming synovitis and fatigue. A severe attack mimics acute arterial embolism and critical/irreversible digital ischaemia, and raises the question of evolution to systemic sclerosis. Reassuring features are bilateral symmetric cold-triggered episodes recovering with warming, strong pulses, no persistently white/necrotic digit, and no sclerodactyly/telangiectasia/ulcers. Telehealth cannot examine pulses/perfusion or joints, but the patient can self-observe finger colour and trial gentle rewarming; the correct approach is rewarming, cold avoidance and simple analgesia, with GP/rheumatology review for a calcium-channel blocker and DMARD consideration, and a clear safety-net (ED) for a single digit staying white/numb despite warming, a black/necrotic tip, or a persistently cold pulseless limb.

differential.evidence_basis: Cold-triggered triphasic Raynaud's with arthralgia on known UCTD.
Screen for a persistently white/numb or necrotic digit.
Screen for a single persistently cold/pulseless limb.
Bilateral warming-responsive attack, no necrotic digit, strong pulses — a Raynaud's flare.
Low probability but retained as a safety-net (unexaminable remotely).
No current sclerodermatous features; monitor and refer.
Working telehealth diagnosis: UCTD/Raynaud's flare managed with rewarming/cold avoidance/analgesia and rheumatology review, with a critical-ischaemia safety-net.

red_flags.description: A single digit staying completely white/numb for >2 hours despite warming, or a black/necrotic finger tip (critical digital ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Critical/irreversible digital ischaemia threatens the digit and needs emergency assessment.

red_flags.description: A single persistently cold, pale, pulseless limb (arterial embolism)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute arterial occlusion is a surgical emergency.

red_flags.description: Cold home / loss of hand function while isolated (perpetuating triggers, self-care risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing cold exposure perpetuates attacks and impairs nutrition/medication access.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for a persistently white/necrotic digit or a cold pulseless limb.
Sending a typical recovering Raynaud's attack to ED by ambulance.
Not warning against very hot water/heating pads on numb fingers (burn risk).
Not arranging rheumatology review/CCB consideration or addressing the cold home.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Recovering triphasic Raynaud's with strong pulses; no persistent white/necrotic digit — not critical ischaemia
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports rewarming/cold-avoidance management with prompt review and a critical-ischaemia safety-net.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen critical ischaemia/embolism; advise gentle rewarming (warm not hot), home warming and gloves, simple analgesia for arthralgia, and arrange GP/rheumatology review (CCB/DMARD); escalate to ED for a persistently white/numb or black digit, or a cold pulseless limb.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing everything to Raynaud's could miss critical ischaemia/embolism; hot-water rewarming could burn numb fingers.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Secondary Raynaud's is managed with cold avoidance and calcium-channel blockers; critical digital ischaemia requires urgent specialist/vascular care.
Assess for progression to defined CTD (serology, capillaroscopy); escalate therapy for severe/refractory Raynaud's.
